food_subgroup: Indian bread and tandoori mixed platter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for naan bread (FOODON_03302051) but no specific entry for a combined naan and tandoori basket platter. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers flat bread as a category. No specific code exists for a combined naan-tandoori basket. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code found for a combined naan and tandoori basket platter.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: One basket serving typically comprises two pieces of naan bread (approximately 120 g) alongside a tandoori protein portion such as chicken or paneer (approximately 180 g), totalling around 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Naan and Tandoori Chicken composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for a combined naan-tandoori basket. Values are derived by weighted composite of ICMR-NIN entries for naan (plain, ~40% weight share) and tandoori chicken (~60% weight share), reflecting a typical restaurant basket composition. Naan per-100g: energy 297 kcal, protein 8.7 g, fat 4.5 g, carbohydrate 55.0 g. Tandoori chicken per-100g: energy 150 kcal, protein 25.0 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 3.0 g. Weighted composite (40:60): energy ~208 kcal, protein ~18.5 g, fat ~4.8 g, carbohydrate ~24.0 g. Sodium estimated from USDA SR Legacy naan and tandoori chicken entries as ICMR-NIN does not consistently report sodium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Naan bread and tandoori composite estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a combined naan-tandoori basket. GI estimated as weighted composite: naan bread GI ~71 (University of Sydney GI Database, white wheat flour naan) at 40% carbohydrate contribution, and tandoori chicken GI ~0 (negligible carbohydrate, protein-dominant) at 60% weight share. Effective GI of the basket is driven primarily by the naan component. Composite GI estimated at approximately 57 (medium), reflecting dilution of naan GI by the low-carbohydrate tandoori protein portion. Glycemic load per 300 g serving: (57 × 24.0 g carb per 100g × 3) / 100 = 41.0 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a full 300 g basket delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one naan piece and a small tandoori portion, as a full basket can contribute more than half the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) driven by refined wheat flour naan; glycemic load per full serving is high at approximately 41 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion of naan (one piece rather than two) and prioritise the tandoori protein component to moderate postprandial glucose response, given the medium GI and high glycemic load of a full basket serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee/butter in naan and skin-on tandoori preparations; moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should request naan prepared with minimal ghee and choose skinless tandoori chicken to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, and limit consumption to one serving per occasion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A Naan and Tandoori Basket is a popular Indian restaurant sharing platter combining freshly baked naan bread with tandoori-cooked items, typically tandoori chicken or paneer. Naan is a leavened flatbread made from refined wheat flour (maida), yogurt, and a small amount of fat such as ghee or butter, baked at high heat in a clay tandoor oven, producing a soft, slightly charred, chewy bread with a mild buttery flavour. The tandoori component consists of protein — most commonly chicken pieces or paneer — marinated in spiced yogurt with cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garam masala, garlic, and ginger, then cooked in the same tandoor oven at temperatures exceeding 400°C, yielding a smoky, charred exterior with a moist interior. As a mixed platter, the basket provides a substantial protein contribution (~18.5 g per 100 g) from the tandoori portion alongside moderate carbohydrate (~24 g per 100 g) from the naan. The glycemic index of the basket is estimated at approximately 57 (medium), driven by the refined flour naan component, with a high glycemic load per full serving (~41 g GL per 300 g basket). Sodium content is moderate to high (~420 mg per 100 g), primarily from spice marinades and any added salt. Clinically, this dish is relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI, high GL per serving), hypertension (sodium load), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from ghee and skin). In Singapore, this dish is commonly served in Indian restaurants and casual dining venues as a starter or shared main course.

What is in a tandoori basket?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for a combined naan-tandoori basket. Values are derived from a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN entries for naan and tandoori chicken at a 40:60 weight ratio reflecting typical restaurant basket composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database naan bread data. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Composite dish: nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN naan and tandoori chicken entries at 40:60 weight ratio. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these components. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these components. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these components. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for naan and tandoori chicken; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this combined basket does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sodium estimated from USDA SR Legacy naan and tandoori chicken entries. Dietary flags reflect a non-vegetarian basket with chicken; a paneer variant would be vegetarian. Halal status assumes halal-certified chicken as is standard in Singapore Indian restaurants. The food_name has been normalised from 'Naan/Tandoori Basket' to 'Naan and Tandoori Basket' for canonical English clarity.
